title: handleDecodeStart registers startPromise AFTER launching the async IIFE, creating a TOCTOU window
description: At line 316 the async IIFE is immediately invoked and may reach `await initBackend()` and resolve synchronously when the backend is already cached (`backend !== null`). In that case the full body (including `decodeSessions.set`) completes before line 352 (`pendingDecodeStarts.set`) runs, leaving a stale completed promise in `pendingDecodeStarts`. Subsequent messages for the same sessionId that arrive before the event loop processes line 352 will hit `hasAnySession` returning false (since `pendingDecodeStarts` is not yet set), allowing a duplicate `decode_start` to create a second handler for the same sessionId — the second handler then overwrites the first in `decodeSessions`. The same race applies to `handleEncodeStart` (lines 359-407).
evidence: `initBackend()` is an `async function` — even when `backend !== null` it returns `Promise.resolve(backend)`, so `await initBackend()` always yields to the microtask queue. The IIFE therefore always suspends at the first `await` before executing any further body, meaning line 352 (`pendingDecodeStarts.set`) always runs synchronously before the IIFE resumes. No synchronous-resolution race is possible in the current code.

title: onCancel calls disposeActiveEncoder(cancelFirst=true) while readEncoderChunks may still be iterating encoder.chunks()
description: `onCancel` calls `encoder.cancel()` then `encoder.dispose()` via `disposeActiveEncoder(reason, true)` at line 146. Concurrently, the `readEncoderChunks` branch of `Promise.all` may still be suspended inside `for await (const chunk of encoder.chunks())`. The `encoder.cancel()` call races with the live async iterator. If the underlying codec does not defend against cancel-while-iterating, this can produce a use-after-free or double-free in native code. The `disposePromise` guard prevents a second `dispose()` call but does not prevent `cancel()` racing with the iterator that `run()` holds open.
evidence: The cancel-while-iterating race is real in structure (disposeActiveEncoder is called while readEncoderChunks may hold an open async iterator). Whether it is safe depends on the NodeEncoder native/WASM implementation's cancel() semantics, which cannot be determined from this file alone. The disposePromise guard prevents double-dispose but not cancel-then-dispose racing an in-flight iterator.

title: onCancel races with readDecoderEvents: both paths call disposeActiveDecoder concurrently without coordination
description: `onCancel` (line 141) fires `void this.disposeActiveDecoder()`. At the same moment `readDecoderEvents` may be awaiting the next event from `decoder.events()`. When `disposeActiveDecoder` nulls `this.decoder` and calls `decoder.dispose()`, the in-flight `for await` in `readDecoderEvents` may receive an error or continuation on a disposed decoder. The `disposePromise` guard prevents a second `dispose()` call, but the first dispose overlaps with an active iterator. This is the same pattern as the browser decode-handler's 'best-effort unblock' comment — but the comment acknowledges the risk rather than resolving it.
evidence: The code comment at line 429 explicitly says 'Best-effort unblock of decoder.events() iterator — mirrors browser handler.' The race is acknowledged by design; safety depends on whether the NodeDecoder implementation's events() async generator handles concurrent dispose() cleanly. Cannot be verified without inspecting the native codec.

title: Single wakeResolve slot means a second concurrent caller to waitForChunk loses its wake signal
description: `waitForChunk` stores exactly one resolve function in `this.wakeResolve`. If two code paths called `waitForChunk` concurrently, the second call would overwrite `wakeResolve`, leaving the first promise permanently unresolved and causing a task leak. In the current codebase only `feedDecoder` calls `waitForChunk`, so the risk is latent but not eliminated by any guard or comment. The same single-slot pattern exists for `waitForResume` / `resumeResolve`. A future refactor or new caller of the internal API could trigger this silently.
evidence: Only `feedDecoder` calls `waitForChunk` and only one coroutine runs `feedDecoder` per DecodeHandler instance (launched in the constructor). There is no concurrent caller today. The 'latent risk' framing is speculative — the private method is not part of any external API, and the class architecture enforces single-caller semantics by design.

title: Message router fires handleDecodeStart and handleEncodeStart with void, leaking their promise on uncaught rejection
description: The `port.on('message', ...)` handler calls `void handleDecodeStart(msg)` and `void handleEncodeStart(msg)`. If either async function throws after its internal try/catch (e.g., an unexpected synchronous throw in `new DecodeHandler()`), the rejection is unhandled and falls through to `process.on('unhandledRejection', ...)`. While that handler does trigger shutdown, it reports a generic `UnhandledRejection` rather than a session-specific `decode_error`, so the caller never receives a failure message for that sessionId and may wait indefinitely. The `void handleShutdown()` calls inside `uncaughtException`/`unhandledRejection` handlers also go unawaited.
evidence: Verified at lines 266-267 and 278-279: `void handleDecodeStart(msg)` and `void handleEncodeStart(msg)` discard rejections. If `new DecodeHandler()` or `new EncodeHandler()` throws (e.g., from a codec bug), the session receives no `decode_error` reply, causing the caller to stall indefinitely. The `unhandledRejection` handler does fire shutdown but posts a generic non-session error message.

title: releaseSessionState removes from decodeSessions before calling onCancel, causing onCancel's onSessionEnd callback to delete a key that no longer exists
description: `releaseSessionState` deletes the session from `decodeSessions` at line 424 and then calls `decode.onCancel('release_state')` at line 426. `onCancel` calls `finishSession`, which calls `callbacks.onSessionEnd(sessionId)`, which calls `decodeSessions.delete(id)` again. The double delete is a no-op on a Map, so it does not corrupt state, but it is semantically wrong: the handler fires the `onSessionEnd` callback for a session that was already removed, which could confuse any future wrapper that relies on the callback signalling the moment of removal. The same issue exists for `encodeSessions`.
evidence: Verified at lines 423-426: `decodeSessions.delete(sessionId)` is called before `decode.onCancel('release_state')`. `onCancel` (decode-handler.ts line 140) calls `finishSession('cancelled')` which calls `callbacks.onSessionEnd` (line 181) which calls `decodeSessions.delete(id)` (worker.ts line 345). Double-delete is a no-op on Map but the onSessionEnd callback fires redundantly after the entry was already removed.

title: doShutdown awaits pending starts then iterates decodeSessions, but new sessions created by those starts are not cancelled
description: `doShutdown` awaits `Promise.allSettled([...pendingDecodeStarts.values(), ...pendingEncodeStarts.values()])`. If a pending start resolves and creates a new handler during that await, the handler is added to `decodeSessions` *after* the snapshot was taken. The subsequent `for (const h of decodeSessions.values())` loop runs after the allSettled resolves, so in practice it will see the new handlers. However the snapshot of `pendingDecodeStarts.values()` is evaluated eagerly at the spread site, so any start that is *added* after the spread but before the allSettled resolves is not awaited. Since `shuttingDown = true` is set before the await, `handleDecodeStart` will still accept the message but abort before creating the handler — so the practical risk is low but the ordering guarantee is fragile.
evidence: The guard at line 339 (`if (shuttingDown || cancelledPendingStarts.delete(msg.sessionId))`) ensures that any pending start that resolves while `shuttingDown=true` immediately exits without creating a handler. Therefore no new handlers can be added to `decodeSessions` during the `allSettled` await, and the subsequent iteration catches all handlers.

title: No cancellation-token abstraction: cancellation state scattered across four boolean flags
description: `DecodeHandler` tracks cancellation state via `cancelled`, `ended`, `paused`, and the `state` enum — all checked independently at many call sites via `isTerminal()`. `EncodeHandler` duplicates the same pattern with `cancelled`, `finished`, `ended`, and `state`. A shared `CancellationToken` or `AbortSignal`-like object would centralise the check, make it impossible to miss a flag, and give a single awaitable for callers that need to know when cleanup is truly complete. The scattered flags have already produced one historical bug (the slot-leak on abort-before-async-scheduler noted in MEMORY.md).
evidence: Verified: four separate state booleans (`cancelled`, `ended`, `paused`, `inputClosed`) plus the `state` enum are individually guarded at 10+ call sites. The historical slot-leak bug (MEMORY.md: 'seam freed decode slot on header/preview/recon mid-decode') confirms the pattern has caused real bugs. A centralized token would reduce the surface.

title: EncodeHandler.run() has no catch on Promise.all — encoder errors silently drop
description: DecodeHandler.run() wraps its Promise.all in try/catch and calls failSession on unhandled errors. EncodeHandler.run() has only a finally block (line 176-178) with no catch. If feedEncoder or readEncoderChunks throws an unexpected exception, the error is re-thrown through the finally into the .catch at construction time (line 92), which calls failSession('Internal', String(err)). This path works, BUT the finally disposes the encoder BEFORE failSession is called from the .catch, meaning the encoder is already null when failSession wakes waitForPixels — which is fine — but the error message is stringified from whatever value was thrown, losing the stack trace. More critically, if both legs of Promise.all reject, only the first rejection is routed to failSession; the second rejection is suppressed by Promise.all semantics with no logging.
evidence: Verified at lines 174-178: `try { await Promise.all([...]) } finally { ... }` with no `catch`. Compare decode-handler lines 226-231 which has an explicit `catch (err)` that calls `failSession`. If both feedEncoder and readEncoderChunks reject simultaneously, `Promise.all` propagates only the first rejection; the second is silently swallowed.

title: null as any initializer for startPromise creates a window where Map holds a null value
description: Both handleDecodeStart (line 315) and handleEncodeStart (line 370) assign `let startPromise: Promise<void> = null as any` before the IIFE sets the real value. Between the assignment of null and the IIFE completing synchronously to set startPromise, any code path that reads pendingDecodeStarts.get(sessionId) will receive undefined (because set happens at line 352 after the IIFE is constructed), but the identity comparison `pendingDecodeStarts.get(msg.sessionId) === startPromise` on line 321 and 337 would compare against null if the catch block executes before the outer assignment at line 352 runs. Since this is all synchronous JS before the first await, the race cannot actually occur at runtime, but the pattern is fragile and was evidently an oversight (the comment 'superseded' at line 336 makes sense only when the promise is real).
evidence: Line 316 assigns `startPromise = (async () => {...})()` — the IIFE return value (the Promise) is captured immediately into `startPromise` before any async body runs. The identity check at line 321 inside the catch uses the already-assigned real Promise. Line 352 `pendingDecodeStarts.set` does run after the IIFE starts but the `await initBackend()` always yields first, so the set runs before the catch can fire. No null comparison is possible.

title: void worker.terminate() in ready-timeout handler discards termination errors
description: When the ready-timeout fires at line 49-56, `void worker.terminate()` is called and any rejection from Worker.terminate() is silently discarded. If terminate() fails (e.g., the worker is in an uninterruptible state or the process has already exited), the caller receives the spawn rejection but the underlying worker may remain running, leaking a thread. The pattern matches legitimate best-effort cleanup, but at minimum the rejection should be logged via onDiagnostic or console.error.
evidence: Verified at line 53: `void worker.terminate()` discards the returned Promise. `Worker.terminate()` in Node.js returns a Promise that can reject if the worker cannot be terminated. A silent failure here leaves a dangling thread while the spawn caller only sees the timeout error.

title: void worker.terminate() in shutdown timeout handler discards errors
description: Inside the shutdown() method, when the graceful-shutdown timer fires (line 74), `void worker.terminate()` again discards errors. If terminate() rejects here, the shutdownPromise resolves successfully (res() is still called) while the worker may still be running. This could leave dangling threads, particularly problematic in test runners or process pools that rely on clean worker termination.
evidence: Verified at lines 74-78: `void worker.terminate()` followed immediately by `res()`. If terminate() rejects (thread stuck), the shutdown resolves successfully anyway, misreporting clean termination.

category: error information leakage in user-facing message
title: JXL_FORCE_NATIVE error message leaks full internal diagnostic trace to caller
description: When JXL_FORCE_NATIVE=1 and native load fails, the thrown error (line 41-43) includes the full diagnostics array joined with newlines, which may contain internal paths, module resolution errors, native addon stack traces, or other system-level details. This is intentional for debugging but could be a concern in environments where the Error message propagates to end-user logging or telemetry. Lower severity since this is a startup/configuration error, not a runtime user-data error.
evidence: This error is thrown during worker startup for a configuration failure (JXL_FORCE_NATIVE=1). The error propagates to the parent process that spawned the worker, which is a trusted internal component. This is not user-facing data and the diagnostic detail is appropriate for an operator-facing configuration error.

title: routeDecodeMessage silently drops messages for unknown non-pending sessions
description: If a decode_chunk/decode_close/decode_cancel/decode_pause/decode_resume message arrives for a sessionId that neither has an active handler nor a pending start (e.g., the session was already cleaned up or was never started), routeDecodeMessage (line 217-224) silently returns without error. This means late-arriving messages after session teardown are silently swallowed, which is expected for cancel/close but could mask logic bugs where chunks arrive for an unknown session. A log or metric at debug level would help diagnose ordering issues.
evidence: Silent drop of late messages for cleaned-up sessions is correct and expected behavior. The protocol is designed for cancel/close messages to be idempotent and safe to drop after teardown. Adding errors here would cause spurious session-error messages during normal graceful teardown races. A debug log would be nice but this is not a bug.

title: routeEncodeMessage silently drops messages for unknown non-pending sessions
description: Same issue as errors-6 but for the encode path. encode_pixels/encode_finish/encode_cancel arriving for an unknown sessionId are silently discarded with no diagnostic. Since encode_pixels can carry large pixel buffers (the transferred ArrayBuffer was already detached by the sender), losing it silently can result in an encode that hangs waiting for pixels that never arrive.
evidence: If encode_pixels arrives for an unknown session, the session has already terminated (encode_done or encode_error was sent). The caller's encode session should have already handled that terminal message. The transferred ArrayBuffer being dropped is not a live encode hanging — the encode is already over. Same reasoning as errors-6.

title: feedDecoder does not call decoder.close() when inputClosed is set while paused
description: In feedDecoder, after waitForResume() returns (the session was resumed), the loop iterates again and calls waitForChunk(). If `inputClosed` was set while the session was paused and there are no more chunks, the inner while loop is skipped and the condition `this.inputClosed && !this.isTerminal() && !this.paused` at line 311 fires, calling decoder.close(). This appears correct, BUT if cancel arrives while paused (onCancel sets cancelled=true and calls wake()/wakeResume()), feedDecoder wakes from waitForResume, the `continue` at line 293 re-enters the top of the loop, sees `isTerminal()` is true, and exits WITHOUT calling decoder.close(). The decoder's events() generator thus never sees EOF. disposeActiveDecoder() (called in the finally of run()) should unblock events(), but only if dispose() cancels the async generator — not guaranteed by the NodeDecoder interface contract.
evidence: Verified in feedDecoder (lines 289-315): when cancel fires while paused, `onCancel` sets `this.paused = false` (line 135) and calls `finishSession` which sets `this.ended=true`. `wakeResume()` is called, feedDecoder wakes from `waitForResume`, loops back to `while (!this.isTerminal())`, sees `cancelled===true`, exits without calling `decoder.close()`. The NodeDecoder interface contract (line 49) does not guarantee that `dispose()` terminates the `events()` async iterator.

title: sidecarOffsets accumulates entries for every chunk index below sidecarCount, not just actual sidecar boundaries
description: sidecarOffsets is built by pushing totalBytes for every chunk when chunkIndex < sidecarCount (lines 356-358). This means if sidecarCount is, say, 3 and the encoder emits 100 chunks, only 3 entries are pushed — correct. But if the encoder emits fewer chunks than sidecarCount (e.g., small image, sidecarCount=5, chunks=2), sidecarOffsets will have 2 entries rather than 5, silently producing a mismatched manifest. There is no validation that sidecarOffsets.length === sidecarCount before including it in the done message.
evidence: Verified at lines 354-359 and 375-379: if the encoder emits fewer chunks than sidecarCount, `sidecarOffsets.length` will be less than `sidecarCount` with no validation before inclusion in `doneMsg`. The condition at line 379 `sidecarOffsets.length > 0` would still include the mismatched array.

title: EncodeHandler.run() does not call endSessionOnce() on error path — session may never be removed from Map
description: If run() throws unexpectedly (e.g., codec.createEncoder() throws at line 170 before this.encoder is set), the finally block calls disposeActiveEncoder() (which resolves immediately since encoder is null), then the .catch at construction (line 92) calls failSession(). failSession calls endSessionOnce() only if not already cancelled/done/error (line 386). But if the state has not yet transitioned to 'error' (because the error happened before any state change), failSession sets state='error' and calls endSessionOnce(). This path is OK. However if createEncoder() throws AFTER line 171 sets this.encoder but BEFORE the try block at line 174, the finally disposes the encoder but failSession is called through the .catch with String(err) — which does call endSessionOnce. The only gap is if port.postMessage itself throws in failSession (already-closed port during shutdown): endSessionOnce would not be called and the session would leak in the Map.
evidence: The `.catch` at line 92 calls `failSession('Internal', String(err))`. `failSession` (lines 385-393) calls `endSessionOnce()` unless already cancelled/done/error. The only identified gap (port.postMessage throwing) is an extremely edge case during shutdown where the session Map cleanup is already being handled by `doShutdown`. The normal error paths are covered.

title: No sentinel error type or error-code registry — code/message string pairs are informal
description: failSession() takes a free-form (code: string, message: string) pair. Codes like 'Internal', 'QueueOverflow', 'CapabilityMissing' are scattered across decode-handler, encode-handler, and worker.ts with no shared enum or constant registry. This makes exhaustive error handling on the consumer side brittle and makes typos in codes undetectable at compile time. A shared ErrorCode const enum or string literal union in @casabio/jxl-core/protocol would catch mismatches at build time.
evidence: Verified: error codes 'Internal', 'QueueOverflow', 'CapabilityMissing', 'DuplicateSession', 'BackpressureOverflow' appear as string literals scattered across worker.ts, decode-handler.ts, and encode-handler.ts with no shared registry. A typo in any code string would silently produce a non-standard error code to consumers.

title: No watchdog / inactivity timeout on decode sessions — a stuck backend hangs forever
description: If the NodeDecoder backend enters a hung state (e.g., events() never yields and never rejects, or push() never returns), feedDecoder and readDecoderEvents will await indefinitely. There is no per-session inactivity deadline beyond the optional budgetMs (which only checks elapsed wall time in between events, not inside a blocked push). A watchdog timer that fires failSession after N seconds of no event/drain activity would bound the hang window. The same gap exists in the encode path.
evidence: Verified: `budgetMs` is checked at line 433 only via `performance.now() - this.stageStartMs > this.opts.budgetMs`, which is called only between events in readDecoderEvents and between chunks in feedDecoder. If `decoder.push()` or `decoder.events()` blocks indefinitely, `checkBudget()` never fires and the session hangs forever with no timeout.

title: Startup backend init failure silently reports 'wasm' as backend type — consumer cannot distinguish init-failed from wasm-loaded
description: In the startup IIFE (lines 508-519), if initBackend() throws, the catch block silently sets backendType to 'wasm' (the default) and posts worker_ready. The consuming code in spawn.ts will resolve the handle as ready with backend='wasm', but any subsequent decode/encode session will fail with 'CapabilityMissing'. There is no way for the caller to know at spawn time that both backends failed. The worker_ready message has no 'error' or 'warning' field to carry this state. A worker_ready with a 'backendError' field (or a separate worker_error before worker_ready) would let the host fail-fast at spawn time rather than at first session use.
evidence: Verified at lines 508-519: the catch block is empty and `backendType` remains 'wasm'. `worker_ready` is posted with `backend: 'wasm'` even when both backends failed. The pool will accept this worker as healthy and route sessions to it, which then fail with 'CapabilityMissing', causing one spurious failure per session before the pool detects misconfiguration.

title: defaultImportNative and defaultImportWasm swallow ALL import errors silently via .catch(() => null)
description: Both defaultImportNative (line 101) and defaultImportWasm (line 108) use `.catch(() => null)` to suppress any import error. This means syntax errors, wrong exports, version incompatibilities, or even OOM during module evaluation are all collapsed to null and reported only as 'Failed to import @casabio/jxl-native (returned null)'. The actual import error is discarded and never reaches the diagnostics array. tryNative/tryWasm catch blocks also capture thrown errors (line 69-73), so double-catching is possible. The .catch inside the import should at minimum capture the error and pass it to onDiagnostic before returning null.
evidence: Verified at lines 101 and 108: `.catch(() => null)` discards the import error entirely. The caller in tryNative (line 59-61) only checks for null and logs 'Failed to import @casabio/jxl-native (returned null)' without the actual error reason. A syntax error or ABI mismatch in the native addon would produce this same opaque message.

title: adaptiveHwm divides by (pushLatencyEma + 10) — safe from div-by-zero but EMA stays at 0 until first push, producing max HWM regardless of actual latency
description: The EMA starts at 0 (line 94) and is only updated after a push completes (line 306). Before the first push, adaptiveHwm() returns floor(HWM_BASE * min(2.0, 120/10)) = floor(6*2) = 12. This means the first maybePostDrain() call after queuing chunks fires with HWM=12 even if the queue has more than 6 items but fewer than 12, potentially sending a premature drain signal. This is a minor functional issue but could cause unnecessary scheduler re-scheduling during cold start. Not a crash risk.
evidence: HWM=12 before first push means the drain fires when queueDepth < 12 AND queuedBytes < BYTE_DRAIN_HWM. A higher HWM is more permissive (drain allowed more often), which is conservative-safe for cold start: the scheduler gets told it can send more chunks sooner. This is not 'premature' — it's an appropriate cold-start default that causes no regression.

title: MsgDecodeFinal and MsgDecodeProgress sent without folded metrics declared in protocol (outputBytes, timeToFinalMs, timeToFirstPixelMs, copyMs, copiedBytes)
description: The protocol definition in jxl-core/protocol.ts declares optional folded-metric fields on MsgDecodeFinal (outputBytes, timeToFinalMs, timeToFirstPixelMs, copyMs, copiedBytes) and on MsgDecodeProgress (timeToFirstPixelMs, copyMs, copiedBytes). These are described as 'carried on the frame to avoid separate metric IPCs' and 'the session re-emits each present field'. The node decode-handler never sets any of these fields — it emits all metrics via separate postMetric() calls instead. Consumers that rely on folded metrics (e.g. jxl-session's metric-fold path) will receive none from the node worker, silently producing gaps in telemetry. The browser worker presumably does populate these fields (or both are broken), causing divergent consumer behavior across platforms.
evidence: Verified: protocol.ts (lines 111-135) declares optional fields outputBytes, timeToFirstPixelMs, timeToFinalMs, copyMs, copiedBytes on MsgDecodeFinal, documented as 'folded per-frame metrics to avoid separate metric IPCs.' The node decode-handler at lines 397-410 constructs MsgDecodeFinal without any of these fields, instead posting separate metric messages. Any consumer expecting folded metrics gets none from the node worker.

title: sidecarOffsets logic uses chunkIndex-vs-sidecarCount wrong: records totalBytes after each of the first N chunks, not at sidecar boundaries
description: The protocol comment for MsgEncodeDone.sidecarOffsets says 'Length === sidecarSizes.length when sidecars were emitted'. The handler records a new offset entry for every chunk whose chunkIndex < sidecarCount, but chunkIndex is a monotonically incrementing counter for every encoder chunk (including non-sidecar chunks). The intent is to record the byte boundary after each sidecar thumbnail chunk, but the condition `if (chunkIndex < sidecarCount)` fires for the first N chunks regardless of whether those chunks are actually sidecars. If the encoder happens to emit non-thumbnail data in the first N chunks the recorded offsets will be wrong. Additionally, totalBytes is accumulated before the push, meaning the recorded offset is the running total after including that chunk's bytes — this is actually correct per the spec comment ('cumulative byte offsets at sidecar boundaries'), but the lack of any semantic separation between sidecar and non-sidecar chunks is a fragile contract that can drift silently when encoder chunk ordering changes.
evidence: The sidecarOffsets recording assumes the encoder emits sidecars as the first N chunks. Whether this is a protocol invariant guaranteed by the codec or a fragile assumption depends on the NodeEncoder codec contract, which is not visible in this file. If the encoder guarantees first-N-chunks-are-sidecars, this is correct; if not, it is wrong.

category: missing input validation on public entry point
title: onChunk accepts a shared ArrayBuffer view and silently copies it — but if view spans only part of a shared buffer the copy is correct while the size accounting is not
description: When chunk is a Uint8Array/Buffer that is a sub-view of a larger ArrayBuffer, the code constructs a new Buffer using the view's byteOffset and byteLength — this is correct for the data copy. However, in the overflow check on line 110 it uses chunk.byteLength (the view's length), which is correct. The concern is that a SharedArrayBuffer view passed from a worker (legal in node worker_threads with SAB) will NOT be correctly handled: Buffer.from(sab, offset, length) creates a copy in node >= 16, so this path is safe. However there is no explicit guard or comment noting SharedArrayBuffer is accepted/rejected. The public contract (spec Section 15.2 comment on line 106) says 'Accept both Buffer and Uint8Array' but is silent on SharedArrayBuffer. If a caller passes a SharedArrayBuffer view, the behavior (copy vs. view) depends silently on node version.
evidence: The COOP/COEP header requirement for SharedArrayBuffer means SAB is only available in browser-like environments. In Node.js worker_threads, the protocol uses message-transfer (detached ArrayBuffer) not SAB. The type signature `ArrayBuffer | Uint8Array | Buffer` excludes SharedArrayBuffer at the type level. The actual SAB behavior difference is a theoretical concern not applicable to the current protocol usage.

category: invariant break / enum not handled by all consumers
title: NodeDecodeEvent 'budget_exceeded' from codec is forwarded to postBudgetExceeded which then calls finishSession — but the state machine also sets state='progressive' on the preceding 'progress' event, leaving budget_exceeded reachable from a non-'progressive' state when fired on the first event
description: The state machine transition on line 365 sets this.state = 'progressive' only inside the 'progress' case. If the codec emits 'budget_exceeded' as its very first event (before any 'progress'), this.state is still 'created' or 'headers'. The postBudgetExceeded guard checks this.ended (line 450) but not state, so it proceeds normally. The finishSession call sets state to 'budget_exceeded' after the message is posted. This path works correctly in practice, but the isTerminal() check on line 162 does not include 'created' or 'headers' in its terminal set, which means if the codec backend fires budget_exceeded while the handler is in 'headers' state the handler is not yet in a terminal guard and isTerminal() returns false — consistent with expected behavior. The real concern: the 'budget_exceeded' state is listed in the DecodeState union but NOT checked in isTerminal(). Instead, the 'budget_exceeded' terminal condition is inferred via this.ended. Any future code that checks state directly (not isTerminal() or this.ended) could miss the budget_exceeded case.
evidence: The code excerpt contradicts the finding: `isTerminal()` at lines 162-169 DOES include `this.state === 'budget_exceeded'`. The finder's claim that budget_exceeded is not in isTerminal() is factually wrong. The remaining concern (future code checking state directly) is speculative with no current bug.

title: postWithPixels unconditionally posts the underlying ArrayBuffer of the Buffer — if the native codec returns a Buffer that shares its backing store with a larger arena, the entire arena is transferred, not just the pixel region
description: postWithPixels checks whether the Buffer's byteOffset is 0 AND byteLength equals ab.byteLength. If the Buffer is a slice (byteOffset != 0 or byteLength < ab.byteLength), it calls ab.slice() to extract an exact copy. However, if the Buffer happens to own the full backing ArrayBuffer (byteOffset=0, byteLength==ab.byteLength) but that buffer was allocated with extra capacity (e.g. a native binding that over-allocates and then sub-selects by length), the entire ArrayBuffer — including the excess capacity — is transferred. The consumer receives a pixel buffer of the correct logical size (msg.pixels.byteLength === pixels.byteLength) only if msg.pixels is reassigned before post. In the transfer path on line 441 msg.pixels is still set to pixels (Buffer) before postMessage; the spec says pixels: ArrayBuffer (transferred). The Buffer itself cannot be transferred — only its .buffer can. After transfer the Buffer object is detached, but since Buffer extends Uint8Array the transfer is of pixels.buffer. The pixel data shape seen by the consumer is pixels.buffer.byteLength which may be > the logical frame size.
evidence: The fast path at lines 440-443 transfers `ab` (the full backing ArrayBuffer) when `pixels.byteOffset === 0 && pixels.byteLength === ab.byteLength`. If a native codec over-allocates and sub-selects by length alone (not byteOffset), the full oversized buffer would be transferred. Whether this happens depends on the codec implementation. The guard is correct for the standard Node.js Buffer allocation pattern but may be insufficient for custom native allocators.

title: onCancel skips endSessionOnce when state is already 'done' or 'error', but does not guard against 'cancelled' state re-entry before the cancelled flag is set
description: onCancel checks `this.cancelled || this.state === 'done' || this.state === 'error'` at the top. However it does not check `this.state === 'cancelled'`. The state is set to 'cancelled' on line 139 (this.state = 'cancelled') and then endSessionOnce() is called. But between the guard check and the state assignment, a re-entrant call (possible if the first onCancel awaits disposeActiveEncoder which is async) would pass the initial guard since cancelled is not yet true. The cancelled boolean is set on line 138 before the state, so in practice the re-entry guard works via the boolean. However the state field check in the guard is missing 'cancelled', making the defensive contract weaker than the parallel encode conditions checked elsewhere (see line 96-102 in onPixels which does check state === 'cancelled').
evidence: `this.cancelled = true` is set on line 137 synchronously before any `await`. The re-entry guard `if (this.cancelled || ...)` uses `this.cancelled` which is set in the same synchronous block. JavaScript is single-threaded; no re-entrant call can execute between line 136 and line 137. Missing `state === 'cancelled'` in the guard is cosmetically inconsistent but functionally safe.

title: Message router silently drops messages for sessions that are not active and not pending — no error is reported to the caller
description: In routeDecodeMessage (line 217) and routeEncodeMessage (line 234), if a message arrives for a sessionId that is neither in the active session map nor in the pending-starts map, it is silently dropped with no diagnostic or error response. This can happen when a decode_chunk or encode_pixels arrives after the session was cleaned up (e.g. after onSessionEnd fired but before the host noticed). From the caller's perspective the chunk was accepted but never processed. The contract of the public message interface does not document this silent-drop semantics, and there is no corresponding 'session not found' error message defined in the protocol. This differs from the duplicate-session guard which does emit an error (lines 305-313).
evidence: Silent drop of late messages after session teardown is correct protocol behavior. The session terminal message (decode_done/decode_error/decode_cancelled) has already been sent before the session is removed from the map. The caller should handle teardown upon receiving those terminal messages. Emitting errors for late chunks would create spurious error messages during normal graceful teardown.

category: missing input validation on public entry point
title: resolveCodecModule only inspects two candidates (value and value.default) — ESM re-exports with multiple default wrappings (e.g. value.default.default) will be silently rejected with no diagnostic
description: The candidates array is fixed to `[value, value.default]`. Some bundled native addons (especially when compiled from CJS to ESM via tools like esbuild or rollup) produce double-default wrappings (value.default.default). If neither the top-level nor the single .default candidate is a valid CodecModule, resolveCodecModule returns null and the backend falls back to WASM, with no diagnostic identifying double-wrapping as the cause. The diagnostic messages say only 'Candidate is missing createDecoder/createEncoder' which gives no hint to the user that a deeper unwrap might succeed.
evidence: The @casabio/jxl-native and @casabio/jxl-wasm packages are internal packages under project control. Double-default wrapping would be a known build artifact of those specific packages, not an unpredictable external module. The two-candidate check handles the standard CJS-to-ESM wrapping pattern. Double-default is an edge case that would be caught by integration tests when packages are updated.

title: handleDecodeStart initializes startPromise with `null as any` before the IIFE assigns it — if initBackend() resolves synchronously the pendingDecodeStarts identity check fires with null, causing the session to be silently abandoned
description: On line 315, `let startPromise: Promise<void> = null as any`. The IIFE is assigned to startPromise on lines 316-352. If `initBackend()` resolves in the same microtask (e.g. when backend is already initialized), the `if (pendingDecodeStarts.get(msg.sessionId) === startPromise)` check on line 336 uses the correct resolved promise because `startPromise` is assigned before the IIFE body runs. However, the assignment on line 352 (`pendingDecodeStarts.set(msg.sessionId, startPromise)`) happens AFTER the IIFE starts executing. If a synchronous initBackend resolution were possible, by the time the check runs startPromise would already be set from the outer let. In practice initBackend is always async (Promise chain), so this race cannot fire in the current code. But the `null as any` initialization is a contract fragility: any future refactor that makes initBackend synchronous (e.g. cached sync path) would silently break the identity guard. The pattern is repeated in handleEncodeStart (lines 370-371).
evidence: Line 316: `startPromise = (async () => {...})()` assigns the IIFE's returned Promise to `startPromise` synchronously. The IIFE body starts executing but suspends at `await initBackend()` (always yields since initBackend is async). By the time any await-continuation fires, `startPromise` holds the real Promise and `pendingDecodeStarts.set` has run. The `null as any` is never observable at any identity check.

title: No contract test verifying that node and browser decode-handlers produce identical message sequences for the same input
description: The jxl-core protocol is described as 'same protocol as jxl-worker-browser/decode-handler.ts' (decode-handler.ts line 3), but there are no cross-platform parity tests. The handlers.test.ts exercises only the node handler with fake backends. A shared contract test fixture that drives both the browser and node decode-handlers with the same fake codec events and asserts identical message sequences would catch divergences like the missing folded-metric fields (contracts-1) before they reach production. The test infrastructure (fakePort, waitFor) is already in place in both packages — a shared protocol-parity test module could be extracted.
evidence: The finding is accurate: no cross-platform parity test exists, and contracts-1 (missing folded metrics) demonstrates exactly the kind of divergence such a test would catch. The comment at decode-handler.ts line 3 ('Same protocol as jxl-worker-browser/decode-handler.ts') establishes the parity contract but there is no automated verification of it.

title: Incoming MainToWorkerMessage is trusted without runtime shape validation — malformed messages from test harnesses or future protocol additions cause silent undefined-field access
description: The message router casts the incoming object directly to MainToWorkerMessage and immediately dispatches on msg.type. There is no runtime check that required fields (e.g. sessionId, chunk) are present and of the correct type. A missing sessionId would cause Map lookups with undefined as the key, silently creating a session under the key 'undefined'. A missing chunk on a decode_chunk message would cause onChunk(undefined) which would fail later with an obscure error. Adding a narrow guard (at minimum checking typeof msg === 'object' && msg !== null && typeof msg.type === 'string') would make contract violations observable.
evidence: Worker MessagePort messages come from a trusted internal caller (jxl-session/pool) that constructs messages via TypeScript typed interfaces. Runtime schema validation adds complexity with negligible security/correctness benefit in a trusted IPC channel. A missing `sessionId` would cause `Map.get(undefined)` → `undefined` → silently dropped (not a Map insertion), which is safe.

title: sidecarOffsets records end-of-chunk byte positions, not start positions
description: totalBytes is incremented (line 354) BEFORE sidecarOffsets.push(totalBytes) (line 357), so each entry records the cumulative byte position at the END of the sidecar chunk rather than its start offset. Consumers expecting start offsets (e.g. to slice the assembled output) will be off by the chunk's byte length. If the intent is end offsets the field name 'sidecarOffsets' is misleading and the consumer must compensate. In the browser encode-handler the same pattern should be audited for consistency.
evidence: Verified at lines 354-357: `totalBytes += buffer.byteLength` then `sidecarOffsets.push(totalBytes)`. The pushed value is the cumulative bytes AFTER the sidecar chunk, i.e., the end boundary. The field name 'sidecarOffsets' and typical offset semantics (start byte positions) suggest start offsets were intended. Consumers slicing by these values as start offsets would read wrong regions.

title: Crashed-worker exit handler emits worker_shutdown_ack to all handlers even when shutdown was never requested
description: On a non-zero exit code, the 'exit' event handler (lines 138-150) dispatches both a worker_error AND a worker_shutdown_ack to ALL current messageHandlers. If shutdown() was never called, no cleanup timer exists to clear, yet the synthetic worker_shutdown_ack may confuse any handler that treats it as a protocol signal. The ack is dispatched in a second separate loop (lines 146-150), which also bypasses the restoration of 'prev' handlers that shutdown()'s internal handler would perform, leaving messageHandlers in a potentially inconsistent state. The ack should only be emitted when a graceful shutdown was actually in-flight.
evidence: Verified at lines 138-150: on non-zero exit code, `worker_shutdown_ack` is emitted to all `messageHandlers` regardless of whether `shutdown()` was called. The `shutdown()` method (lines 70-97) installs a temporary handler that intercepts `worker_shutdown_ack` and restores the previous handlers. When a crash occurs without a prior `shutdown()` call, this synthetic ack bypasses the normal handler-restoration logic, leaving `messageHandlers` in the crash state.

title: checkBudget uses strict '>' so a session exactly at budgetMs boundary is not treated as exceeded
description: checkBudget() returns false when elapsed == budgetMs exactly, meaning the budget is considered not-exceeded at the boundary. Using '>=' would be more correct and consistent with the contract that budgetMs is the maximum allowed elapsed time. This is a minor boundary condition that could allow one extra frame to be emitted when elapsed equals budgetMs. The browser decode-handler should be checked for the same condition.
evidence: `performance.now()` has sub-millisecond precision; the probability of hitting exactly `elapsed === budgetMs` is infinitesimally small. Whether `>` vs `>=` is semantically correct depends on whether `budgetMs` is intended as 'strictly less than' or 'at most'. Without a spec definition of this boundary, this cannot be confirmed as a bug.

title: Messages for active sessions silently dropped during shutdown instead of being routed
description: The message router early-returns for all messages (except worker_shutdown) once shuttingDown is true. This means decode_cancel or encode_cancel messages that arrive concurrently with an in-progress shutdown are silently discarded without being routed to their handlers. The doShutdown() function already cancels all active sessions (lines 456-457), so in practice the handlers are cancelled, but any race where a cancel arrives AFTER shuttingDown=true but BEFORE doShutdown() runs its cancellation loop results in the cancel being a no-op (handler not yet cancelled, message dropped). Low severity because doShutdown() covers the gap, but the ordering assumption is fragile.
evidence: Verified at line 262: all non-shutdown messages are dropped when `shuttingDown=true`. `doShutdown()` (line 446) sets `shuttingDown=true` before the `allSettled` await. Any cancel message arriving after this gate but before `doShutdown` calls `h.onCancel()` at line 456 is dropped, and the session handler remains running until `doShutdown` reaches it. The gap window is the duration of the `allSettled` await.

title: isLoadedBinding returns true when binding has no 'probe' function — fails open
description: The function only invokes the probe check when typeof value['probe'] === 'function'. If the binding object has no probe property at all, the function skips all checks and returns true. This means a binding object with neither probe nor createDecoder/createEncoder (e.g. an empty {} or a partially-constructed object) passes isLoadedBinding. The subsequent isCodecModule check catches the missing createDecoder/createEncoder, but the diagnostic path will attribute the failure to 'missing createDecoder/createEncoder' rather than 'binding not loaded', masking the real problem. The guard should fail-closed when probe is expected but absent, or the comment should clarify that probe is optional.
evidence: Verified at lines 152-174: when `value['probe']` is not a function (including when probe is absent), `isLoadedBinding` returns `true` unconditionally. An empty object `{}` or any object without a probe function passes. The diagnostic 'Candidate is missing createDecoder/createEncoder' is then issued by the caller, which misattributes the failure to a missing codec method rather than an unloaded binding.

title: onCancel sets state='cancelled' before endSessionOnce() but run() may still be active and observes stale state
description: onCancel() sets this.state = 'cancelled' (line 138), calls wake() (line 139), then posts encode_cancelled (lines 141-144), then calls endSessionOnce() (line 145), then disposeActiveEncoder (line 146). However the run() coroutine is concurrently executing feedEncoder and readEncoderChunks. The run() finally block calls disposeActiveEncoder() (line 177) unconditionally. Since disposeActiveEncoder is idempotent (uses disposePromise guard), this is safe, but endSessionOnce() is NOT idempotent under concurrent access — if run()'s finally somehow calls endSessionOnce() before onCancel's call (not possible here, but the ordering is fragile). More concretely: endSessionOnce() calls callbacks.onSessionEnd(sessionId) which deletes the session from the map. If onCancel() is called while run()'s Promise.all is still live, the session is removed from the map mid-run, and any subsequent failSession call inside feedEncoder would also call endSessionOnce() but the ended guard prevents double-fire. This ordering is functionally safe but the state machine has a race window between state='cancelled' and ended=true where external callers see inconsistent state.
evidence: JavaScript is single-threaded. `onCancel` executes synchronously up to `void this.disposeActiveEncoder(...)`. The `run()` coroutine is suspended at an `await` point. The `endSessionOnce()` in `onCancel` runs before `run()` can resume. The `ended` guard in `endSessionOnce()` prevents double-fire. No real race window exists in single-threaded JS.

title: worker_ready always reports backendType='wasm' on initBackend failure, even if WASM was never successfully selected
description: At startup (lines 508-519) the worker reports backendType='wasm' as a default even when initBackend() throws. If both native and WASM backends fail to load, the worker reports backend='wasm' in worker_ready, giving the pool a false impression of capability. The first actual session will fail with CapabilityMissing, but the worker_ready signal has already been used to mark the worker as available. This causes one spurious session failure per worker before the pool detects the misconfiguration. The comment acknowledges this but the backendType default should be 'none' or the worker should post a diagnostic indicating degraded state.
evidence: Same as errors-13. Verified at lines 508-519: when initBackend() throws (both backends failed), `backendType` defaults to 'wasm' and `worker_ready` is posted with `backend: 'wasm'`. The pool accepts the worker as healthy. The comment at line 514-515 acknowledges the behavior but the consequence (one spurious CapabilityMissing per session until pool retires the worker) is a real operational defect.

title: feedDecoder has no assertion that decoder.close() is called exactly once
description: The feedDecoder loop calls decoder.close() when inputClosed is set and the loop exits via return (line 312-313). If the loop exits via isTerminal() becoming true (e.g. from a cancel) AND inputClosed was already true, close() is never called. This is correct behavior — cancellation supersedes close — but there is no invariant check that prevents close() from being called after cancellation if the loop somehow reaches line 311 in a future refactor. An assertion (if !this.isTerminal()) before close() would make the contract explicit and guard against future regression.
evidence: The condition at line 311 already includes `!this.isTerminal()` as a guard before calling `decoder.close()`. The invariant the finder wants to add is already present. The finding describes adding a guard that already exists in the code.

title: Buffer.from in onPixels uses chunk.byteLength for ArrayBuffer path but chunk is narrowed to Uint8Array for byteOffset
description: The Buffer.from call on line 114 uses a single-expression ternary for offset and length. For the non-ArrayBuffer branch (Uint8Array/Buffer), `(chunk as Uint8Array).byteOffset` and `(chunk as Uint8Array).byteLength` are used. However chunk could also be a Buffer (which is a Uint8Array subclass), and for Buffer the byteOffset into the underlying ArrayBuffer may not be 0. This is handled correctly (Buffer.isBuffer test is not done first, so Buffer falls through to the Uint8Array branch). The issue is that the same pattern in decode-handler.ts (line 115-119) is cleaner with explicit isAB branching, while encode-handler.ts uses an inline ternary that is harder to audit. Not a functional bug in current Node.js where Buffer.from(ab, off, len) works — but a maintenance risk.
code_excerpt: const buf = Buffer.from(chunk instanceof ArrayBuffer ? chunk : chunk.buffer, chunk instanceof ArrayBuffer ? 0 : (chunk as Uint8Array).byteOffset, chunk instanceof ArrayBuffer ? chunk.byteLength : (chunk as Uint8Array).byteLength);
evidence: The inline ternary is functionally correct: the same logic as decode-handler's explicit isAB branching but inlined. Buffer.isBuffer is not needed because Buffer is a Uint8Array subclass, so the Uint8Array branch (`chunk.buffer`, `chunk.byteOffset`, `chunk.byteLength`) handles Buffer inputs correctly. This is a style/readability issue, not a bug.

title: Full diagnostic chain including internal error stacks leaked in thrown Error message
description: When JXL_FORCE_NATIVE=1 and native backend fails, the thrown Error embeds the full diagnostics array which includes `err.stack` from caught exceptions (line 70: `err.stack || err.message`). Stack traces reveal internal file system paths, Node.js version, and native addon layout. In a multi-tenant or server-side scenario the caller could surface this to the end user.
evidence: This error is thrown during worker startup (a startup configuration failure) and propagates only to the internal process that spawned the worker — a trusted component. This is not a user-facing error surface. Stack traces in startup errors for internal worker configuration failures are standard operator-level diagnostics.

title: Full diagnostic chain including internal stacks leaked in 'neither backend' thrown Error
description: The fallback error thrown when neither native nor WASM backend loads similarly appends the full diagnostics array, which can include captured `err.stack` strings from both import attempts. These stacks may contain local file paths and internal package layout details.
evidence: Same as security-1: this error is thrown during startup and is propagated only to the internal parent process. It is not user-facing. Diagnostic detail in startup errors for internal workers is appropriate for operators.

title: Full exception stack trace posted over worker MessagePort on uncaughtException
description: The `uncaughtException` and `unhandledRejection` handlers call `formatError(err)` which returns `err.stack ?? err.message`. The resulting message is posted as a `worker_error` event to the parent port. If the parent port is proxied through a web API or logged verbatim, internal file paths and library internals in the stack trace could be exposed to a less-trusted consumer.
evidence: Worker MessagePort messages go to the parent process (the pool manager) — a trusted internal component. This is not an external API surface. Stack traces in worker_error messages are necessary for diagnosing crashes in production. The risk of these propagating to end-users depends on application-level logging decisions, not this code.

title: No cap on total active sessions — unbounded Map growth enables OOM DoS
description: The `decodeSessions`, `encodeSessions`, `pendingDecodeStarts`, and `pendingEncodeStarts` Maps have no total-count cap. A sender that fires many concurrent `decode_start` / `encode_start` messages (each with a distinct sessionId) will keep growing these Maps and their associated handler objects until OOM. Per-session byte and message caps exist, but there is no cap on the total number of live sessions.
evidence: Verified: no session count limit exists anywhere in worker.ts. `handleDecodeStart` at line 305 only checks for duplicate sessionIds via `hasAnySession`, not for total session count. An attacker controlling the message stream could create thousands of concurrent sessions until OOM. The pool (external) may limit this, but the worker itself has no defense.

title: 128 MiB per-session queue cap is per-DecodeHandler instance, not global across all sessions
description: MAX_QUEUED_BYTES (128 MiB) is enforced per DecodeHandler instance. With N concurrent decode sessions each allowed 128 MiB, total worker memory consumption scales as 128*N MiB. Since there is no cross-session cap (see security-4), an attacker controlling sessionId strings could force N sessions to simultaneously queue 128 MiB each.
code_excerpt: const MAX_QUEUED_BYTES = 128 * 1024 * 1024; // 128 MiB safety cap — see browser handler
evidence: Verified: MAX_QUEUED_BYTES at line 34 is a module-level constant applied per-handler. No cross-session byte accounting exists. This is a valid amplification of security-4 — the total memory limit scales with N unbounded sessions.

title: isRecord accepts any non-null object including arrays and prototype-polluted objects
description: The `isRecord` guard uses `typeof value === 'object' && value !== null`, which also returns true for Arrays, Dates, Buffers, etc. Downstream code then reads arbitrary string keys (e.g., `value['createDecoder']`, `value['loadNativeBinding']`) without any own-property check. A crafted imported module that returns an Array or prototype-polluted object could manipulate property lookup. Severity is low because the module source is a trusted package resolved by Node's module loader.
evidence: The imported modules (@casabio/jxl-native, @casabio/jxl-wasm) are internal packages under project control resolved by Node's module system from the local filesystem. Prototype pollution would require a compromised package, which is a supply-chain attack already noted under security-8. The isRecord pattern is standard TypeScript narrowing and not a meaningful attack vector here.

title: No schema validation on MainToWorkerMessage before routing — malformed messages silently accepted
description: All incoming messages are cast directly from the `message` event to `MainToWorkerMessage` and routed without runtime schema validation. A misrouted or malformed message (e.g., a message missing `sessionId`, or with an unexpected `type`) is either silently dropped or passed to a handler that may behave unpredictably. A Zod or manual runtime-check guard at the switch entry point would reject invalid messages before they reach session state.
evidence: The worker MessagePort receives messages from the internal pool/session layer, which constructs messages via TypeScript-typed interfaces. Runtime schema validation for internal trusted IPC adds complexity with minimal benefit. `Map.get(undefined)` → `undefined` is silently handled as 'not found' — it does not create a new entry. Unknown `msg.type` values fall through to the `default: break` case at line 296 with no effect.

title: No CI dependency-audit step to detect compromised @casabio/jxl-native or @casabio/jxl-wasm packages
description: The worker dynamically imports `@casabio/jxl-native` and `@casabio/jxl-wasm` at runtime. These are optional packages loaded without integrity verification. A supply-chain compromise of either package would give attacker code access to the worker's Node.js process. Adding `npm audit` or `bun audit` to CI and pinning package hashes in the lockfile would reduce this risk.
evidence: @casabio/jxl-native and @casabio/jxl-wasm are internal packages in this monorepo (not public npm packages). Supply-chain compromise of internal packages is a general organizational security concern, not a code-level finding for this file.

title: Buffer.from() copy on every incoming chunk when view already covers the full ArrayBuffer
description: onChunk() always constructs a new Buffer wrapping the incoming data via Buffer.from(chunk.buffer, byteOffset, byteLength). When the caller passes a Node.js Buffer that already aligns to the full backing ArrayBuffer (byteOffset===0, byteLength===ab.byteLength), Buffer.from with three arguments still allocates a new Buffer header object instead of returning the same buffer. The fast-path check present in postWithPixels (lines 440-443) is absent here, so every chunk enqueue costs a redundant allocation and an isAB branch even when the input is already a Buffer.
evidence: `Buffer.from(arrayBuffer, byteOffset, length)` creates a Buffer that views the existing backing ArrayBuffer without copying the underlying data. The 'new Buffer header' is a thin wrapper object (~40 bytes), not a data copy. The chunks here are typically KiB-to-MiB data; the header allocation is negligible compared to the data size and is not a meaningful hot-path concern.

title: Long inline Buffer.from() expression in onPixels() forces allocation for every pixel chunk even when input is already a Buffer
description: onPixels() rebuilds a Buffer from the incoming chunk unconditionally using a triple-argument Buffer.from call. When the input is already a Node.js Buffer that spans its full backing ArrayBuffer this still allocates a new Buffer header object. Additionally the very long inline expression (line 114) duplicates chunk.byteOffset and chunk.byteLength reads three times each, making it hard to audit correctness. A Buffer.isBuffer fast-path (like the pattern used in toBuffer()) would eliminate the allocation for the common case.
code_excerpt: const buf = Buffer.from(chunk instanceof ArrayBuffer ? chunk : chunk.buffer, chunk instanceof ArrayBuffer ? 0 : (chunk as Uint8Array).byteOffset, chunk instanceof ArrayBuffer ? chunk.byteLength : (chunk as Uint8Array).byteLength);
evidence: Same as performance-1: `Buffer.from(ab, offset, len)` is a view, not a data copy. The header allocation is negligible. The `toBuffer()` helper at line 397 already has the Buffer.isBuffer fast-path pattern — but it returns the same Buffer instance without copying. The net difference is one header object vs zero header objects per call, immaterial in the pixel-processing hot path.

title: ab.slice() in postWithPixels copies the pixel buffer when view doesn't own the entire ArrayBuffer
description: When a Buffer's byteOffset is nonzero or byteLength is shorter than the backing ArrayBuffer, postWithPixels falls back to ab.slice() which always allocates a new ArrayBuffer and copies all bytes. For streaming decoders that emit views into a large internal buffer this triggers a full pixel-buffer copy on every progress frame before transfer. The copy is necessary for the transfer semantics, but if the codec is under the project's control an alternative design could ensure every emitted Buffer already owns its backing store, eliminating this path entirely.
evidence: Verified at lines 438-447: `ab.slice()` copies the full pixel data when the Buffer is a sub-view. For a 12MP image this is ~36 MiB copied per progress frame. The fast path at line 440-443 avoids the copy for owned buffers. Whether the native codec emits owned or sub-view buffers determines whether this path is hit in production.

title: adaptiveHwm() recomputes Math.max/min/floor on every chunk push from feedDecoder
description: adaptiveHwm() is called inside maybePostDrain() which is called after every decoder.push() in the inner loop of feedDecoder. The result depends only on pushLatencyEma, which changes slowly via EMA. Caching the computed HWM and invalidating it when the EMA changes by more than a threshold (or simply recomputing lazily once per drain interval) would avoid up to N Math.max/min/floor calls per decoded image where N is the number of chunks.
evidence: `Math.max`, `Math.min`, and `Math.floor` are single CPU instructions. The EMA update before `maybePostDrain` already executes float arithmetic. The overhead of three math calls per chunk is orders of magnitude below the `decoder.push()` WASM call cost. This is not a meaningful optimization opportunity.

title: performance.now() called twice per chunk push (one for timing, one inside maybePostDrain)
description: feedDecoder records t0=performance.now() before decoder.push(), then calls maybePostDrain() which immediately calls performance.now() again to get 'now'. These two calls are logically adjacent; passing the post-push timestamp from feedDecoder into maybePostDrain would eliminate one syscall per chunk without changing semantics.
evidence: Verified at lines 303-308 and maybePostDrain (lines 323-348): `performance.now()` is called at line 305 (post-push) for EMA, then called again inside `maybePostDrain()` at line 325 for drain interval check. The two calls are adjacent in time (microseconds apart). Passing the post-push timestamp into `maybePostDrain` would eliminate one `performance.now()` syscall per chunk. Minor but straightforward.

title: performance.now() called twice per pixel push in feedEncoder (timing + maybePostDrain)
description: Same pattern as decode-handler: performance.now() is called at t0 before encoder.pushPixels(), then again inside maybePostDrain() via 'const now = performance.now()'. The post-push timestamp from the outer call could be reused, saving one syscall per tile/chunk.
evidence: Same pattern as performance-5, verified in encode-handler.ts lines 269-275: `performance.now()` at line 269 (pre-push t0), then post-push at line 271, then `maybePostDrain()` at line 275 calls `performance.now()` again (line 296). One redundant syscall per pixel chunk pushed.

title: ab.slice() pixel copy in postChunk when Buffer is a view into a larger ArrayBuffer
description: postChunk has the same buffer-alignment guard as postWithPixels in decode-handler. When a codec emits chunks as views into a shared ring or growable buffer, every chunk triggers ab.slice() which allocates a new ArrayBuffer and copies N bytes before transfer. This is structurally the same issue as performance-3 and has the same mitigation path.
evidence: Verified at lines 316-324: same pattern as performance-3. `ab.slice()` at line 321 copies the entire chunk data when the Buffer is a sub-view of a larger backing store. This would trigger a full data copy per encoded chunk in the streaming path.

title: hasAnySession() performs six Map.has() lookups on every decode/encode message route check
description: hasAnySession() is called only at session-start time (handleDecodeStart/handleEncodeStart), not on the hot message path, so this is low-frequency. However the function checks six separate Map structures in order. Given that in steady state (backend warmed, sessions active) both pendingDecodeStarts and pendingEncodeStarts are almost always empty, checking them last instead of first would short-circuit faster in the common case. This is a minor ordering micro-optimization.
evidence: `hasAnySession()` is called only at session-start time (cold path), not on the per-message hot path. Six Map.has() lookups on a cold path with small maps is negligible. The ordering micro-optimization has no measurable impact.

title: messageHandlers.slice() called twice per non-zero exit code event, allocating two temporary arrays
description: On worker exit with a non-zero code (lines 139-151), the exit handler calls messageHandlers.slice() twice in succession to iterate and dispatch two separate synthetic messages. Since the slice is only needed to safely iterate while messageHandlers might be mutated, a single slice captured once into a local variable would suffice, saving one array allocation per crash event. This is extremely cold path but is a straightforward cleanup.
evidence: Verified at lines 139-150: two separate `slice()` calls on a worker crash. This is a crash handler — an extremely cold path. The second slice ensures any handlers added/removed by the first iteration's callbacks are excluded from the second pass, which is the correct defensive behavior. Collapsing to one slice would change the iteration semantics. Not a real optimization target.

title: No throughput or latency benchmarks for node worker handlers — regressions are invisible in CI
description: The handler tests verify correctness (message types, metric names, cancellation suppression) but do not measure chunk throughput, time-to-first-pixel, or memory allocation counts. The adaptive HWM and EMA-based drain logic exist precisely to tune throughput, but there is no automated test that would catch a regression in those values. Adding a single benchmark test with a synthetic codec emitting N chunks would make throughput regressions detectable without requiring a full integration harness.
evidence: The adaptive HWM and EMA drain tuning logic has no corresponding benchmark. A regression in drain coalescing (e.g., HWM computation drift) would only be caught by end-to-end performance degradation, not unit tests. The test infrastructure (fakePort, fake codec) is already in place.
